items.q: Is recursion just another name for a loop?
items.correct: No - recursion solves a problem through self-calls that each add a stack frame, while iteration repeats within one frame; they can compute the same things but work differently.
items.misconception: Yes - the compiler treats recursion and loops as identical.
items.random_wrong: Yes - recursion is a loop that runs backwards.
items.why: Both repeat work, so they merge in students' minds. The call stack is the key difference: deep recursion consumes stack memory and can overflow where a loop would not.

items.q: Are global variables good practice because every subroutine can reach them?
items.correct: No - that universal reach is the danger: any part of the program can change them, creating hidden dependencies and bugs; passing parameters keeps interfaces explicit.
items.misconception: Yes - using globals everywhere makes programs simpler and safer.
items.random_wrong: Yes - local variables are slower so globals should be preferred.
items.why: Convenience is mistaken for quality. Uncontrolled shared state makes testing and debugging far harder, which is why exam mark schemes reward parameter passing.

items.q: Is an IF statement a form of iteration?
items.correct: No - IF is selection: it chooses between paths once; iteration constructs such as WHILE and FOR repeat statements multiple times.
items.misconception: Yes - IF repeats its block until the condition becomes false.
items.random_wrong: Yes - selection and iteration are the same construct.
items.why: WHILE also tests a condition, so the constructs blur. The difference is repetition: WHILE loops back to retest, IF tests once and moves on.

items.q: The three fundamental programming constructs are:
items.why: Correct answer: Sequence, selection and iteration. “Input, output and storage” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Every recursive subroutine must have a base case because otherwise it would:
items.correct: Call itself indefinitely until the call stack overflows
items.why: Correct answer: Call itself indefinitely until the call stack overflows. “Run faster than intended” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A local variable declared inside a function can be accessed:
items.why: Correct answer: Only within that function. “From anywhere in the program” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which computational method repeatedly halves a problem, solves the parts, and combines the results?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Divide and conquer. “Backtracking” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A loop is written as FOR i = 1 TO 10. How many times does the loop body execute?
items.why: The real figure is 10 iterations. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
